lessons.title: Object relationships

exercises.title: Composition: Room owns a Light
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Model a composition relationship where a `Room` fully owns a `Light`. The light is created when the room is created and destroyed when the room is destroyed. Print messages that trace the lifecycle.

## Input

- A single line containing a string `name` (1-50 characters, no spaces) representing the room name.

## Output

Exactly three lines:
1. `Light created for <name>`
2. `<name>: light is on`
3. `Light destroyed for <name>`

where `<name>` is the room name read from input.

## Requirements

- Define a class `Light` that takes a `const std::string&` room name in its constructor and stores it.
- `Light`'s constructor prints `Light created for <name>`.
- `Light`'s destructor prints `Light destroyed for <name>`.
- `Light` has a public `void turnOn() const` member function that prints `<name>: light is on`.
- Define a class `Room` that has a `Light` member variable (composition -- not a pointer). `Room`'s constructor takes a `std::string` name and initializes the `Light` with that name.
- `Room` has a public `void illuminate() const` method that calls `turnOn()` on its light.
- In `main`, read the room name, create a `Room` on the stack, and call `illuminate()`.

test_cases.label: Kitchen room
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Light created for Kitchen
Kitchen: light is on
Light destroyed for Kitchen

test_cases.label: Bedroom room
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Light created for Bedroom
Bedroom: light is on
Light destroyed for Bedroom

test_cases.label: Garage room
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Light created for Garage
Garage: light is on
Light destroyed for Garage

exercises.title: Aggregation vs dependency
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Model a `Team` that **aggregates** `Player` objects (the team references players but does not own their lifetime) and has a **dependency** on a `std::ostream` for printing.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `n` (1 <= n <= 10), the number of players.
- Lines 2 to n+1: each line contains a single string `name` (1-50 characters, no spaces).

## Output

- `Team roster:` followed by one line per player in the format `- <name>`.
- A final line: `Players still exist` (proving the players outlive the team).

Example for input `2 / Alice / Bob`:
```
Team roster:
- Alice
- Bob
Players still exist
```

## Requirements

- Define a class `Player` with a `std::string` name, a constructor taking `const std::string&`, and a `const std::string& getName() const` accessor.
- Define a class `Team` that stores an array of `const Player*` pointers (use a fixed-size array of 10 `const Player*` initialized to `nullptr`, plus an `int` count). This is aggregation -- `Team` does not `new` or `delete` any `Player`.
- `Team` has `void addPlayer(const Player& p)` that stores the address of `p`.
- `Team` has `void printRoster(std::ostream& out) const` that prints `Team roster:` then each player as `- <name>`. The `std::ostream&` parameter is a dependency.
- In `main`, create players in an array, build a team inside an inner scope so it is destroyed first, then print `Players still exist` to prove the players outlive the team.

test_cases.label: Two players
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Team roster:
- Alice
- Bob
Players still exist

test_cases.label: Single player
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Team roster:
- Charlie
Players still exist

test_cases.label: Four players
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Team roster:
- Ana
- Kai
- Leo
- Mia
Players still exist

lessons.title: Composition

exercises.title: Build a composed creature
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Implement two classes, `Point2D` and `Creature`, where `Creature` composes a `Point2D` as a member to represent its location. This demonstrates composition: the `Point2D` is owned by the `Creature` and has no independent existence.

## Input

- Line 1: a string `name` (no spaces, 1-50 characters).
- Line 2: two integers `x` and `y` (-1000 <= x, y <= 1000).

## Output

- Line 1: `<name> is at (<x>, <y>)`

where `<name>`, `<x>`, and `<y>` are the values read from input.

## Requirements

- Define a class `Point2D` with private `int` members `m_x` and `m_y`, constructed via a two-parameter constructor.
- `Point2D` must have public getters `getX()` and `getY()` (both `const`).
- Define a class `Creature` with private members `std::string m_name` and `Point2D m_location`.
- `Creature`'s constructor takes `std::string_view name` and `const Point2D& location`.
- `Creature` must have public `const` getters `getName()` (returns `std::string_view`) and `getLocation()` (returns `const Point2D&`).
- In `main`, read input, construct a `Creature`, and print the output using the getters.

exercises.title: Computer with composed parts
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Model a `Computer` that composes a `CPU` and a `Memory` part. The `Computer` owns both components and exposes a `printSpecs()` method that prints a summary. This exercise reinforces that composed parts are value members whose lifetimes are tied to the whole.

## Input

- Line 1: a string `cpuModel` (no spaces, 1-50 characters) and an integer `cores` (1 <= cores <= 128).
- Line 2: an integer `sizeGB` (1 <= sizeGB <= 1024).

## Output

- Line 1: `CPU: <cpuModel> (<cores> cores)`
- Line 2: `Memory: <sizeGB>GB`
- Line 3: `Computer built`

## Requirements

- Define a class `CPU` with private members `std::string m_model` and `int m_cores`. Provide a constructor and a `void print(std::ostream& out) const` method that prints `<model> (<cores> cores)` to the given stream (no newline).
- Define a class `Memory` with a private member `int m_sizeGB`. Provide a constructor and a `void print(std::ostream& out) const` method that prints `<sizeGB>GB` to the given stream (no newline).
- Define a class `Computer` with private members `CPU m_cpu` and `Memory m_memory`. Provide a constructor that takes a `CPU` and a `Memory`. Provide a `printSpecs()` const method that prints the three output lines using the `print` methods of each part.
- In `main`, read input, construct the parts, construct the `Computer`, and call `printSpecs()`.

test_cases.label: Typical desktop
test_cases.expected_stdout:
CPU: Ryzen7 (8 cores)
Memory: 16GB
Computer built

test_cases.label: Single core minimal
test_cases.expected_stdout:
CPU: i3 (1 cores)
Memory: 4GB
Computer built

test_cases.label: Server config
test_cases.expected_stdout:
CPU: EPYC (128 cores)
Memory: 1024GB
Computer built

lessons.title: Aggregation

exercises.title: Department lead lookup
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Model a simple aggregation: a `Department` that holds a reference to a `Teacher` it does not own. Print the department name and its lead teacher's name.

## Input

- Line 1: the teacher's name (a single word, no spaces).
- Line 2: the department name (a single word, no spaces).

## Output

- A single line: `<department>: <teacher>`

where `<department>` is the department name and `<teacher>` is the teacher name.

## Requirements

- Define a class `Teacher` with a `std::string` member set via the constructor and a `getName()` const accessor returning `const std::string&`.
- Define a class `Department` that stores a `const Teacher&` (aggregation -- it does not create or destroy the teacher). It also stores a `std::string` name. Provide a `print()` const member function that outputs the required line.
- In `main`, create the `Teacher` first, then the `Department`, then call `print()`. The teacher must outlive the department.

exercises.title: Shared teacher across departments
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Demonstrate that a single `Teacher` can belong to multiple aggregates simultaneously. Create two departments that share the same teacher, print each department's info, then confirm the teacher still exists after both departments go out of scope.

## Input

- Line 1: the teacher's name (a single word).
- Line 2: two department names separated by a space (single words, no spaces within names).

## Output

- Line 1: `<dept1> lead: <teacher>`
- Line 2: `<dept2> lead: <teacher>`
- Line 3: `<teacher> still exists`

## Requirements

- Reuse the `Teacher` class from Exercise 1 (constructor + `getName()`).
- Define a `Department` class that stores a `std::string` name and a `const Teacher&`. Provide a `print()` const member function that outputs `<dept> lead: <teacher>`.
- In `main`, create one `Teacher`. Inside an inner block scope, create two `Department` objects referencing that same teacher. Call `print()` on each. After the inner block ends (both departments destroyed), print `<teacher> still exists` to prove the teacher outlives the departments.

test_cases.label: Teacher shared across two departments
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Science lead: Garcia
Engineering lead: Garcia
Garcia still exists

test_cases.label: Different names
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Biology lead: Park
Chemistry lead: Park
Park still exists

test_cases.label: Short names
test_cases.expected_stdout:
AI lead: Wu
ML lead: Wu
Wu still exists

lessons.title: Association

exercises.title: Doctor-Patient Association
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Model a unidirectional association between `Doctor` and `Patient`. A doctor keeps track of which patients they see, but patients do not track their doctors.

## Input

Line 1: an integer `D` — the number of doctors (1 <= D <= 10).
Next `D` lines: each contains a doctor name (single word, no spaces).
Line after doctors: an integer `P` — the number of patients (1 <= P <= 10).
Next `P` lines: each contains a patient name (single word).
Line after patients: an integer `A` — the number of associations.
Next `A` lines: two integers `d p` (0-indexed) meaning doctor `d` sees patient `p`.
Final line: a single integer `q` — a doctor index to query.

## Output

Print the queried doctor's name followed by a colon. If the doctor has patients, print a space then the patient names separated by spaces (e.g. `Alice: Charlie Eve`). If the doctor has no patients, print only the name and colon with nothing after it (e.g. `DrJones:`).

End output with a newline.

## Requirements

- Create a `Patient` class that stores a name and provides `getName()`.
- Create a `Doctor` class that stores a name and a `std::vector<Patient*>` for associations.
- Provide `Doctor::addPatient(Patient*)` and `Doctor::getName()`.
- Neither class should create or destroy the other — both are created in `main`.
- Print patient names in the order they were added to the doctor.

exercises.title: Bidirectional Student-Course Association
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Model a bidirectional association between `Student` and `Course`. A student can enroll in multiple courses, and a course tracks which students are enrolled. Neither object owns the other.

## Input

Line 1: an integer `S` — number of students (1 <= S <= 10).
Next `S` lines: a student name (single word).
Next line: an integer `C` — number of courses (1 <= C <= 10).
Next `C` lines: a course name (single word).
Next line: an integer `E` — number of enrollments.
Next `E` lines: two integers `s c` (0-indexed) meaning student `s` enrolls in course `c`.
Final line: a single integer `q` — a course index to query.

## Output

Print the course name followed by a colon and a space, then the count of enrolled students, then a space, then all enrolled student names separated by spaces. If the course has no students, print the course name, a colon, a space, then `0`.

End output with a newline.

## Requirements

- `Student` stores a name and a `std::vector<Course*>` of enrolled courses.
- `Course` stores a name and a `std::vector<Student*>` of enrolled students.
- Write a free function `void enroll(Student& s, Course& c)` that updates both sides of the association.
- Forward-declare whichever class is needed so both can reference each other.
- Print student names in the order they enrolled in that course.

lessons.title: Dependencies

exercises.title: Format a temperature
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Write a `Thermometer` class whose `display` member function depends on a `Formatter` object to print a temperature. `Formatter` is passed by const reference -- it is not stored as a member.

## Input

- Line 1: a single integer `temp` (-100 <= temp <= 100), the temperature in Celsius.

## Output

- A single line: `Temperature: <temp>C`

where `<temp>` is the integer value read from input.

## Requirements

- Define a class `Formatter` with a public const member function `void format(std::ostream& out, int value) const` that prints the integer value followed by `C` to the given stream (no newline).
- Define a class `Thermometer` with a private `int m_celsius` member set via the constructor.
- `Thermometer` must have a public member function `void display(const Formatter& fmt) const` that prints `Temperature: `, then calls `fmt.format(std::cout, m_celsius)`, then prints a newline.
- `Thermometer` must **not** store the `Formatter` as a member -- this is a dependency, not an association.
- In `main`, read the temperature, construct both objects, and call `display`.

exercises.title: Scoreboard reporter
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Build a `Scoreboard` class that tracks a player's score and a free function `report` that accepts the scoreboard as a dependency to print a summary. The `report` function demonstrates a dependency in the opposite direction -- a function depending on an object it does not own.

## Input

- Line 1: a string `name` (no spaces, 1-50 characters) -- the player's name.
- Line 2: an integer `n` (1 <= n <= 20) -- the number of score entries.
- Line 3: `n` space-separated integers (0 <= each <= 1000) -- the individual scores.

## Output

- Line 1: `Player: <name>`
- Line 2: `Rounds: <n>`
- Line 3: `Total: <sum>`

where `<sum>` is the sum of all scores.

## Requirements

- Define a class `Scoreboard` with private members for the player name and a total score (an `int`, not an array).
- Provide a constructor that takes the player name and initializes the total to 0.
- Provide `void addScore(int points)` that adds to the running total, and `int getRounds() const` that returns how many times `addScore` was called.
- Provide `const std::string& getName() const` and `int getTotal() const`.
- Write a free function `void report(const Scoreboard& sb)` that prints the three output lines. This function depends on `Scoreboard` but does not own it.
- In `main`, read input, call `addScore` for each value, then call `report`.

lessons.title: Container classes

exercises.title: IntArray container
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Implement a simple `IntArray` container class that manages a fixed-capacity dynamic array with `push_back`, `size`, and element access via `get`.

## Input

- Line 1: two integers `capacity` and `n` (1 <= n <= capacity <= 100), the array capacity and the number of elements to add.
- Line 2: `n` space-separated integers (-10000 <= each <= 10000) to push into the array.

## Output

- Line 1: `size: <s>` where `<s>` is the number of elements after all pushes.
- Lines 2 through s+1: each element on its own line, printed from index 0 to size-1.

## Requirements

- Define a class `IntArray` with private members: `int* m_data`, `int m_size`, and `int m_capacity`.
- The constructor takes an `int capacity`, allocates a dynamic array of that size with `new int[capacity]{}`, and initializes `m_size` to 0.
- The destructor calls `delete[]` on `m_data`.
- `void push_back(int val)` adds `val` at index `m_size` and increments `m_size`. Use `assert(m_size < m_capacity)` to guard overflow.
- `int get(int index) const` returns the element at `index`. Use `assert(index >= 0 && index < m_size)` to guard bounds.
- `int size() const` returns `m_size`.
- Include `<cassert>` for the assertions.
- Do not implement copy constructor or copy assignment (the default is fine for this exercise since no copies are made).

test_cases.label: Fill to capacity with mixed values

exercises.title: IntStack with push, pop, and top
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Implement an `IntStack` container class that supports `push`, `pop`, `top`, and `size` operations, then process a sequence of stack commands.

## Input

- Line 1: two integers `capacity` and `q` (1 <= capacity <= 100, 1 <= q <= 100).
- Next `q` lines: each line is one of:
  - `push <val>` where val is an integer (-10000 <= val <= 10000)
  - `pop`
  - `top`
  - `size`

## Output

- For each `top` command: print the top element on its own line.
- For each `size` command: print the current number of elements on its own line.
- For each `pop` command: print the popped value on its own line.
- `push` produces no output.

The input guarantees that `pop` and `top` are never called on an empty stack, and `push` is never called when the stack is full.

## Requirements

- Define a class `IntStack` with private members: `int* m_data`, `int m_size`, and `int m_capacity`.
- Constructor takes `int capacity`, allocates with `new int[capacity]{}`, sets `m_size` to 0.
- Destructor calls `delete[]` on `m_data`.
- `void push(int val)`: asserts not full, stores `val` at `m_data[m_size]`, increments `m_size`.
- `int pop()`: asserts not empty, decrements `m_size`, returns `m_data[m_size]`.
- `int top() const`: asserts not empty, returns `m_data[m_size - 1]`.
- `int size() const`: returns `m_size`.
- Parse commands using `std::string` and `==` comparison.

test_cases.label: Basic push, top, pop, size sequence
test_cases.stdin:
10 7
push 5
push 10
top
size
pop
pop
size

test_cases.label: Interleaved operations with negatives
test_cases.stdin:
10 10
push 1
push -2
push 3
pop
size
push 99
top
pop
pop
pop

lessons.title: std::initializer_list

exercises.title: IntArray with initializer_list constructor
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Implement an `IntArray` class that can be constructed from a braced list of integers using `std::initializer_list`, then print its contents.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `n` (1 <= n <= 20), the number of elements.
- Line 2: `n` space-separated integers (-10000 <= each <= 10000).

## Output

- Line 1: `size: <n>` where `<n>` is the number of elements in the dynamic array.
- Line 2: the dynamic array elements printed space-separated.
- Line 3: `size: 3`
- Line 4: `10 20 30`

## Requirements

- Define a class `IntArray` with private members `int m_size` and `int* m_data`.
- Provide a constructor taking `std::initializer_list<int>` that allocates a dynamic array and copies the elements from the list.
- Provide a second constructor `IntArray(const int* data, int size)` that copies from a raw pointer (already provided in the starter code).
- Provide a destructor that calls `delete[]` on `m_data`.
- Provide `int size() const` and `int operator[](int index) const` public member functions.
- In `main`, read `n` integers into a `std::vector<int>`, then construct an `IntArray` using the pointer constructor. Print its size and contents on lines 1-2.
- Also construct a second `IntArray` from the literal list `{10, 20, 30}` using the `initializer_list` constructor. Print its size and contents on lines 3-4.

test_cases.label: Hidden: five elements with mixed signs

exercises.title: IntArray list assignment and sum
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Implement `operator=` for an `IntArray` class that accepts `std::initializer_list<int>`, allowing the array to be reassigned from a braced list after construction. Then use the reassigned array to compute a sum.

## Input

- A single integer `x` (-10000 <= x <= 10000).

## Output

- Line 1: `before: 1 2 3` (the initial hard-coded contents).
- Line 2: `after: <x> <x*2> <x*3> <x*4>` (four values derived from input, space-separated).
- Line 3: `sum: <s>` where `<s>` is the sum of the four values in the reassigned array.

## Requirements

- The `IntArray` class with an `std::initializer_list<int>` constructor is provided in the starter code.
- Implement `IntArray& operator=(std::initializer_list<int> list)` that deletes the old `m_data`, allocates new storage matching the list size, copies the elements, updates `m_size`, and returns `*this`.
- In `main`, create `IntArray arr{1, 2, 3}` and print its contents prefixed with `before: `.
- Read `x`, then reassign `arr` using `arr = {x, x*2, x*3, x*4}` and print the new contents prefixed with `after: `.
- Compute and print the sum of the reassigned array's elements.

lessons.title: Chapter 23 summary and quiz
lessons.summary_md:
## Chapter 23 Summary

This chapter explored how objects relate to one another, from tight lifecycle coupling to fleeting one-call interactions. Understanding these relationships lets you choose the right coupling level for each design situation.

### Object relationship taxonomy

C++ recognizes four relationship types ranked by strength: composition (strongest), aggregation, association, and dependency (weakest). Each implies different ownership semantics, lifetime rules, and directionality.

### Composition

Composition models a "part-of" relationship where the part cannot exist independently of the whole. The whole creates, owns, and destroys its parts. In C++, composition is implemented with normal member variables. The part's lifetime is bounded by the whole's lifetime. Most class member variables are compositions.

### Aggregation

Aggregation models a "has-a" relationship where the whole references a part but does not manage its lifetime. A `Department` has `Teacher` members, but teachers exist independently. In C++, aggregation is typically implemented with pointers or references to externally-owned objects. The part can belong to multiple wholes simultaneously.

### Association

Association models a "uses-a" relationship with no ownership. Neither side manages the other's lifetime, and the relationship can be bidirectional. Associations are typically implemented with pointers and may involve each side holding a container of pointers to the other.

### Dependencies

A dependency is the weakest relationship: one object temporarily uses another, typically as a function parameter or locally constructed helper. The dependent does not store the dependency as a member.

### Container classes

Container classes hold and organize collections of other objects, handling storage, access, and iteration. `std::vector`, `std::array`, and `std::map` are standard containers. Writing your own container means managing a dynamic array, exposing element access, and tracking size. The container-element relationship is composition when the container owns copies, or aggregation when it holds pointers.

### std::initializer_list

`std::initializer_list<T>` lets a constructor accept a brace-enclosed list of values like `{1, 2, 3}`. Provide a constructor taking `std::initializer_list<T>` to let callers write `MyClass obj{a, b, c}`. The list is lightweight and should be passed by value. When both an `initializer_list` constructor and a matching regular constructor exist, the `initializer_list` version wins for brace initialization.

exercises.title: Playlist with Composition and Initializer List
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Build a `Playlist` class that owns `Song` objects (composition). The playlist accepts songs via `std::initializer_list` at construction and supports adding songs afterward.

## Input
- Line 1: integer N (1 <= N <= 10), the number of songs to add after construction.
- Next N lines: a song title (single word) followed by a space and an integer duration in seconds.

## Output
Print the playlist twice, separated by a blank line. First the initial playlist (3 hardcoded songs), then the playlist after adding N extra songs. Each playlist prints `Playlist:` followed by one line per song: `  <title> (<duration>s)`.

## Requirements
1. `Song` class: `std::string m_title`, `int m_duration`, a constructor, and `void print() const` that outputs `  <title> (<duration>s)\n`.
2. `Playlist` class: stores `std::vector<Song>` (composition). Constructor accepts `std::initializer_list<Song>`. Has `void add(const Song& song)` and `void print() const`.
3. In `main`, construct with `Song{"Alpha", 180}`, `Song{"Beta", 210}`, `Song{"Gamma", 95}`. Print, blank line, read and add N songs, print again.
4. You may include `<iostream>`, `<string>`, `<vector>`, `<initializer_list>`.

test_cases.label: Sample -- add two songs to initial three
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Playlist:
  Alpha (180s)
  Beta (210s)
  Gamma (95s)

Playlist:
  Alpha (180s)
  Beta (210s)
  Gamma (95s)
  Delta (150s)
  Epsilon (300s)

test_cases.label: Hidden -- add no extra songs
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Playlist:
  Alpha (180s)
  Beta (210s)
  Gamma (95s)

Playlist:
  Alpha (180s)
  Beta (210s)
  Gamma (95s)

test_cases.label: Hidden -- add one song
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Playlist:
  Alpha (180s)
  Beta (210s)
  Gamma (95s)

Playlist:
  Alpha (180s)
  Beta (210s)
  Gamma (95s)
  Omega (42s)

exercises.title: School Registry: Container, Aggregation, and Dependencies
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Model a school registry demonstrating three relationship types. `Student` is an independent entity. `Classroom` aggregates students (holds pointers, no ownership). A free function `printRoster` depends on a `Classroom` temporarily.

## Input
- Line 1: integer S (1 <= S <= 10), the number of students.
- Next S lines: a student name (single word) and an integer grade (0-100).
- Next line: integer C (1 <= C <= 3), the number of classrooms.
- Next C groups: a line with the classroom name (single word) and integer K, then a line with K space-separated 0-based student indices.

## Output
For each classroom in input order, print:
```
<Name> roster:
  <Student> (grade: <Grade>)
Average: <avg>
```
where `<avg>` is the truncated integer average. Separate classrooms with a blank line.

## Requirements
1. `Student`: `std::string m_name`, `int m_grade`, constructor, `getName()` and `getGrade()` accessors.
2. `Classroom`: `std::string m_name`, `std::vector<const Student*> m_students`. Has `addStudent`, `getName`, `getStudents`, and `getAverageGrade` members.
3. Free function `void printRoster(const Classroom& room)` prints the roster and average (dependency relationship).
4. In `main`, store students in `std::vector<Student>`, build classrooms with pointers to those students (aggregation), call `printRoster`.
5. You may include `<iostream>`, `<string>`, `<vector>`.

test_cases.label: Sample -- two classrooms with shared students
test_cases.stdin:
4
Alice 90
Bob 75
Clara 88
Dave 62
2
Math 3
0 1 3
Science 2
2 3
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Math roster:
  Alice (grade: 90)
  Bob (grade: 75)
  Dave (grade: 62)
Average: 75

Science roster:
  Clara (grade: 88)
  Dave (grade: 62)
Average: 75

test_cases.label: Hidden -- single classroom with one student
Hidden -- three classrooms with truncated averages
test_cases.stdin:
3
Frank 81
Grace 67
Hank 44
3
History 2
0 2
English 3
0 1 2
Gym 1
1
test_cases.expected_stdout:
History roster:
  Frank (grade: 81)
  Hank (grade: 44)
Average: 62

English roster:
  Frank (grade: 81)
  Grace (grade: 67)
  Hank (grade: 44)
Average: 64

Gym roster:
  Grace (grade: 67)
Average: 67
